topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished mahogany of Dr. Alistair Thorne’s office felt cold beneath my fingertips. I stood by the window, watching the city lights blur into streaks of indifferent neon. He was sitting behind his desk, a calm, almost serene expression on his face, his tailored suit impeccable. Alistair. The boy who used to dismantle my world with a cruel smirk in the schoolyard. Now, he held a degree, a title, and the quiet authority of someone who could mend broken souls. A tight knot tightened in my chest, a familiar, unpleasant clenching. I wanted to shatter that composure, to see the flicker of that old malice return to his eyes. I walked toward him, my steps deliberately slow, each one a calculated encroachment on his space. "You look… different," I managed, my voice rougher than I intended. Alistair looked up, his gaze steady, devoid of surprise, which only sharpened the internal tension coiling within me.

[story 2]

I watched him from across the room, nursing a lukewarm coffee. He was laughing now, a genuine, warm sound that used to terrify me. The memory of his sneer—that sharp, ugly expression he wore when he pushed me against the lockers—flashed behind my eyes. It was a potent, bitter taste. I felt a strange, hot pressure building behind my sternum, a need to disrupt the carefully constructed peace he inhabited. I needed to see the foundations shake. I started pacing the perimeter of the room, my jaw tight. When he finally noticed my presence, he paused his conversation, turning with an almost practiced gentleness. "Are you alright?" he asked, his tone laced with concern, a performance so flawless it made my skin crawl. I stopped pacing, planting my feet firmly on the carpet, refusing to break eye contact. My knuckles were white where I gripped my mug.

[story 3]

The consultation room smelled of sandalwood and quiet understanding. Alistair was leaning forward, his hands open in a gesture of complete vulnerability, something I had never witnessed before. He spoke of trauma, of deep-seated insecurities, and I listened, every word an accusation, every soft inflection a deliberate jab aimed at the boy I used to be. A slow, burning heat began to rise in my throat, pushing against the carefully constructed facade of detached observation I was trying to maintain. I wanted to interrupt the flow, to inject a harsh, ugly truth into his delicate narrative. I leaned back against the wall, crossing my arms tightly over my chest, forcing myself to breathe evenly, though the air felt suddenly too thin. I waited, watching the subtle shift in his expression, calculating the precise moment to strike.
